description: Content Refresh for the below 10 Service Pages. I've added frameworks below as well but I will add all this information into the CMT To request the content as well

title: Add FAQs, categorized by their SEO impact
description:
1. Top Priority: High-Value "Thin" Service Pages
These pages have high traffic potential but lower word counts. Adding 3–5 FAQs here will increase relevant "long-tail" keywords and keep users on the page longer.

https://callthechamps.com/bathroom-remodeling/
Bathroom Remodeling (561 words): Add questions about timelines, permit requirements, and popular fixture brands.

https://callthechamps.com/financing/
Financing (511 words): Add questions about credit score requirements, payment plans, and instant approval processes.

https://callthechamps.com/commercial-plumbing/
Commercial Plumbing (762 words): Add questions about emergency response times for businesses and preventative maintenance contracts.

https://callthechamps.com/water-heaters/
Water Heaters (761 words): Add questions about the lifespan of a heater and signs it’s time for a replacement.

2. Strategic Priority: High-Ticket / Technical Services
These services often have a high "consideration phase" where customers have many technical questions.

https://callthechamps.com/nuflow-technology/
Nuflow Pipe Lining: Add FAQs about how trenchless repair works, its durability compared to traditional digging, and cost-effectiveness.

https://callthechamps.com/slab-leak-repair/
Slab Leak Repair: This is a high-anxiety service. Add FAQs about how you detect leaks without destroying flooring and what insurance usually covers.

https://callthechamps.com/tankless-water-heaters/
Tankless Water Heaters: (specifically this page): Add FAQs comparing tank vs. tankless, annual maintenance needs, and "endless hot water" expectations.

3. Conversational Priority: The "Contact Us" & "Financing" Pages
Users on these pages are close to converting but may have "friction" questions.

https://callthechamps.com/contact-us/
Contact Us: Add FAQs about service call fees, 24/7 availability, and what to expect during the first visit.

https://callthechamps.com/financing/
Financing: Frequently asked questions here help overcome "sticker shock" for major repairs.

4. Local SEO Priority: Major City Landing Pages
You have dozens of city-specific pages (Edmond, Yukon, Moore). Adding a unique FAQ section to the top 5 cities can help differentiate these pages so they don't look like "duplicate content" to Google.

https://callthechamps.com/plumbing-services-edmond/
https://callthechamps.com/plumbing-services-in-norman/
https://callthechamps.com/plumbing-services-in-midwest-city/
https://callthechamps.com/plumbing-services-in-yukon/
https://callthechamps.com/plumbing-services-in-nichols-hills/

Example Question for Edmond page: Do you provide emergency plumbing in Edmond on weekends?

Schema Markup

Make sure they are wrapped in FAQ Schema. This allows the questions and answers to appear directly in the Google Search Results Page (SERP), taking up more "real estate" and pushing competitors further down the page.

Recommended FAQ structure for your service pages:

Question: "How much does [Service] cost in Oklahoma City?"

Question: "What are the signs I need [Service]?"

Question: "How long does a typical [Service] appointment take?"

Question: "Is [Service] covered by my home warranty or insurance?"

title: Restructure Main Menu Navigation
description:
Core Drivers for this Change

1. Scalable Topical Authority (SEO)
Currently, many of our high-revenue services (like Tankless Water Heaters and Nuflow Pipe Lining) are buried within the site architecture. By grouping these into logical parent categories (e.g., Drains & Sewers, Water Quality), we signal to Google that we possess deep topical authority in these specific niches, rather than just being a "generalist" plumber.

2. Improved Link Equity Distribution

Our Screaming Frog audit shows that the Sitemap currently carries a significant weight of internal links. By moving our primary service and location pages into the global navigation, we pass "link juice" directly from the Home Page to our most profitable landing pages, helping them rank higher for competitive local keywords.

3. Enhancing E-E-A-T (Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, Trust)

To separate Champion Plumbing from the competition, we are elevating our "Trust Assets"—such as our Podcast, "In the News" features, and "Champion Cares" community work—into a structured About Us ecosystem. This directly supports Google’s Search Quality Rater Guidelines regarding brand transparency and trust.

4. Frictionless Mobile Conversion

Over 70% of plumbing leads typically originate on mobile devices. The new structure simplifies the mobile interface, ensuring that "Emergency Services" and "Financing" are never more than two taps away.

5. Geographic Visibility

We are creating a dedicated Service Areas hub within the menu. This ensures that users in satellite cities (Yukon, Moore, Norman, etc.) immediately see themselves reflected in our primary navigation, increasing local relevance and decreasing bounce rates from those specific regions.

Next Step after layout is showcased in Basecamp and has approval from client:

I have mapped the new architecture based on current crawl data. Once the menu is live, we will monitor the Crawl Depth of our service pages to ensure they have moved closer to the "root," which should result in faster indexing and improved rankings.

  • Ensured full compliance with semantic SEO and structured-data best practices.


Goal:
Increase topical authority and click-through rates for gas-related plumbing services in Edmond and the Oklahoma City area.

title: Include Sitemap In footer of Client websites
description:
Hi, this is inline w/ a group of tasks we have from a sheet. Here is the link for reference: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1qPfUUC1J2r1s7Ot8Y1PmIcje6WpyMszDhxHGz4TxDTk/edit?gid=0#gid=0

SEO added more info column D. 

I have included the task info here:
--- 
Hey Team!

To enhance our technical SEO baseline, improve crawl efficiency, and ensure consistent signaling across all client properties, It would be great to come to an agreement to outlining a new, standardized protocol for crawl management.

My suggestion is as follows:

Implement the following three directives on all client websites as part of our standard build and maintenance process.

1. XML Sitemap in robots.txt (Primary Directive)

This is the most effective way to inform search engines of all indexable URLs.

Action: Ensure the robots.txt file for every client domain contains a Sitemap directive pointing to the full URL of the XML sitemap.

Implementation Example:
User-agent: 
Sitemap: https://www.clientdomain.com/sitemap.xml

The "Why": This provides a clear, direct path for Google to find and crawl our most important pages, ensuring faster discovery and indexing of new content.

2. User-Facing HTML Sitemap (Footer)

This is for user experience and secondary crawl pathing.

Action: All client websites must have a user-facing HTML sitemap.

Implementation: This sitemap must be linked in the footer of every page on the site.

The "Why": This provides a clear navigation path for users, improves site accessibility, and   acts as a secondary crawl path for search engines to discover deep pages.

Strategic Crawl & Indexation Control

This is critical. We must be precise in telling crawlers what to crawl and what to index.

A. To Control Crawling (Using robots.txt)

We must Disallow crawlers from accessing low-value, non-SEO pages to conserve crawl budget.

Action: Audit and update the robots.txt file to Disallow non-public or utility sections of the site.
Implementation Examples:
Disallow: /wp-admin/

The WP sitemap page  plugin in perfect to use as a plugin for now

Regarding providing a robot.txt:

They differ per client especially regarding disallows but here is a comprehensive one that can be edited according to client needs especially regarding disallows:

User-agent: *

# --- Core WordPress File Disallows ---
# Block all core administrative and include folders.
Disallow: /wp-admin/
Disallow: /wp-includes/

# --- Crawl Budget Optimization ---
# Block non-media plugin and theme files from being crawled.
Disallow: /wp-content/plugins/
Disallow: /wp-content/themes/

# Block internal search result pages, which are low-quality, duplicate content.
Disallow: /?s=
Disallow: /search/

# Block other non-public WordPress files and pages.
Disallow: /wp-login.php
Disallow: /readme.html
Disallow: /trackback/
Disallow: /xmlrpc.php

# --- Allow Directives for Critical Files ---
# Explicitly ALLOW critical AJAX and asset files that Google
# may need for rendering, even if their parent folder is disallowed.
Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php
Allow: /wp-content/uploads/

# --- Sitemap Directive ---
# This is the primary directive to tell all search engines where our XML sitemap is located.
# [IMPORTANT: Replace with the client's actual sitemap URL]
Sitemap: https://www.clientdomain.com/sitemap_index.xml

Also, just an example of placement. Sitemap listed at the very bottom of the footer on the left followed by privacy policy etc.

* In the footer we will use the page /sitemap/ and not the XML sitemap.  The XML sitemap is for Google & the robots.txt.


you can use this as robots.txt:

# --- Core WordPress File Disallows ---
# Block all core administrative and include folders.
Disallow: /wp-admin/

# --- Crawl Budget Optimization ---

Disallow: /wp-content/plugins/

# Block internal search result pages, which are low-quality, duplicate content.
Disallow: /?s=
